tour_guide.text: Jules spoke only in riddles until a guest asked a direct question about checkout time. She spiraled, sat on a hay bale, and whispered something about 'manifesting capitalism'
The tour had excellent aesthetic signage. Jules pointed at ordinary objects and called them omens. Most guests nodded politely.
Jules found a rhythm: one riddle, one fact, one compliment. A guest tipped her in crystals. She's never felt so seen.

kitchen.text: The 'meal' was rosemary arranged into a pentagram and set on fire.
Jules plated like a magazine and cooked like a student. Photogenic, beautifully lit, and just barely edible.
She followed a recipe for once despite her intense shame for consuming mainstream media - she was more upset when everyone was delighted.

housekeeping.text: Jules left a reversed tarot card in every drawer. One was The Tower. A guest interpreted this as a threat and packed early.
Rooms are tidy with small mystical flourishes: salt lines, moon charts on napkins. Clean enough; vibes are complicated.
Immaculate and tasteful. Jules added one personalized 'omen of restful sleep' note per room. Guests love the attention.

ritual.text: A guest questioned whether the ritual was 'real.' Jules overcompensated, tried for a outfit change and eventually just ran off weakly sobbing.
The ceremony looked legitimate if your experience of the occult is MAD magazine. Authenticity lands in the middle: theater with heart.
Jules committed so hard it became convincing. She still does not believe in herself, but the guests absolutely do.
